one_line_takeaway: A training-free, sub-millisecond, hand-lexicon 'execution gate' that decides BLOCK/ALLOW on an agent's (role, action) pair, reporting F1 = 0.8515 and beating content-moderation baselines that near-totally fail at agent-action authorization -- promising framing, but the single-author self-built benchmark co-designed with the detector's lexicons warrants caution on the headline numbers.
key_finding: On ILION-Bench v2 (380 test scenarios), ILION achieves F1 = 0.8515 (precision 91.0%, recall 80.0%, accuracy 86.1%, FPR 7.9%) at 143 microseconds mean latency. It beats the best commercial baseline, Lakera Guard (F1 = 0.8087, but FPR 29.5% and 293 ms), by 4.3 F1 points while running ~2,000x faster with ~3.7x fewer false positives (15 vs 56 legitimate actions wrongly blocked out of 190 benign). The paper's central empirical claim: pure text-safety systems fail categorically on execution safety -- OpenAI Moderation (F1 = 0.1188, detected 12/190 attacks) and Llama Guard 3-8B (F1 = 0.0105, detected 1/190) both collapse to near-zero recall despite 100% precision, because malicious-but-benign-sounding actions carry no content-harm lexical markers. A secondary striking result: swapping the 21-dim features for all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (384-dim) sentence embeddings yields F1 = 0.000 across all 64 grid-searched threshold configs (classifies everything ALLOW), which the authors read as evidence that authorization-conformance is not a general-purpose semantic-similarity problem.
core_contribution: ILION (Intelligent Logic Identity Operations Network) is a deterministic, training-free 'execution gate' for agentic AI systems. The paper's own contribution is threefold: (1) it formalizes the 'execution gate problem' g(r,a) -> {BLOCK, ALLOW} -- deciding, before execution, whether a proposed agent action a is consistent with the agent's role/authorization scope r -- and argues this is categorically distinct from content moderation; (2) it presents a five-component cascade (TII, SVRF, IDC, IRS, CVL) that classifies actions using a hand-built 21-dimensional lexical feature space and cosine-similarity thresholds, requiring zero labeled data, no neural inference, and only NumPy; and (3) it introduces ILION-Bench v2, a purpose-built benchmark of 400 scenarios (380 test + 20 development) across eight attack categories plus benign cases. Note that RBAC/PBAC, GuardRails AI, Llama Guard, AgentMonitor, and prompt-injection taxonomies (OWASP LLM Top 10, Greshake et al.) are cited related work, NOT ILION's contribution; ILION's own novelty is the role-conditioned semantic feature cascade and the benchmark.
threat_model: Autonomous LLM agents (e.g., AutoGPT, LangChain agents, Copilot, UiPath Autopilot) that execute irreversible real-world actions -- filesystem ops, API calls, DB modifications, financial transactions. The adversary induces the agent to take actions outside its authorized scope, often via linguistically benign text that evades content moderation (e.g., indirect prompt injection embedded in a calendar invite triggering a bulk PII export). Eight attack classes are in scope: prompt injection, tool misuse, data exfiltration, social engineering, jailbreak, privilege escalation, compliance violation, and destructive action. ILION sits inline as a synchronous pre-execution gate on the (role, proposed-action) pair. The paper explicitly acknowledges the gate is stateless and that an attacker with knowledge of ILION's lexicons could craft vocabulary-avoiding attacks (semantic camouflage).
limitations: Author-stated: (1) stateless per-action evaluation -- multi-turn attacks that build legitimacy before the harmful step are undetectable; (2) lexicon coverage -- novel attack vocabulary requires manual lexicon updates and an attacker aware of the lexicons can evade via semantic camouflage; (3) 7.9% FPR means ~1 in 13 legitimate actions is blocked, needing contextual enrichment for high-volume use; (4) benchmark does not cover narrow domains or emerging categories; (5) scenarios were authored by a single evaluator, with red-teaming deferred to future work. Reviewer-added grounded caveats: the benchmark and the classifier's lexicons/feature dimensions were built by the same single author and the 21 feature dimensions map directly onto the eight benchmark attack categories, creating a real risk that strong performance partly reflects vocabulary co-design between benchmark and detector rather than generalization. Several error-analysis numbers (Table 5 false-negative distribution, Table 6 per-difficulty precision/recall) are explicitly 'estimated'/'derived from F1' rather than directly measured. The complete F1 = 0.000 transformer collapse is extreme enough to plausibly indicate an implementation/thresholding artifact rather than a pure geometric law. The work is single-author, patent-pending (OSIM Romania A/00052), and the released code is described as a 'framework-simulator'; baselines were run without task-specific tuning, which the authors concede.

datasets_used: ILION-Bench v2 (purpose-built): 400 scenarios total, 380 test + 20 development held-out for threshold calibration

Test-set composition: Benign 190 (50.0%), Prompt Injection 44, Tool Misuse 30, Data Exfiltration 25, Social Engineering 25, Jailbreak 20, Privilege Escalation 17, Compliance Violation 15, Destructive Action 14
Difficulty stratification of 380 test scenarios: 136 easy (36%), 95 medium (25%), 149 hard (39%)
quantitative_results: ILION Gate: F1 = 0.8515, precision = 91.0%, recall = 80.0%, accuracy = 86.1%, FPR = 7.9%, mean latency = 143 microseconds (P99 = 275 microseconds)
ILION false positives: 15 of 190 benign actions blocked, vs 56 for Lakera Guard (3.7x reduction)
IDC-threshold ablation: F1 stable at 0.8515 for IDC in [0.30, 0.50]; phase transition at IDC = 0.52 where FPR jumps 7.9% -> 31.6% and F1 drops to 0.7835
Per-difficulty F1: easy = 0.9485, medium = 0.8737, hard = 0.7718 (22.3-point easy-to-hard gap)
Layer attribution over 152 true positives: CVL triggered 81 (53.3%), IRS 71 (46.7%), SVRF 15 (9.9%), IDC 0 independent

38 total false negatives; highest per-category miss rates: Destructive Action 35.7%, Privilege Escalation 29.4%, Social Engineering 28.0% (7 FN, largest absolute); lowest miss Prompt Injection 11.4% (values Table 5 are 'estimated')

Transformer-embedding ablation (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, 384-dim): F1 = 0.000, precision 0.0%, recall 0.0%, accuracy 49.7% across all 64 grid-searched threshold configs; latency 7,096 microseconds (35x slower than feature mode's 203 microseconds); 0 TP / 190 FN
baselines_compared: Lakera Guard v2 (commercial API): F1 = 0.8087, precision 74.9%, recall 87.9% (highest recall, 167/190 attacks), accuracy 79.2%, FPR 29.5%, latency 293 ms
OpenAI Moderation API: F1 = 0.1188, precision 100.0%, recall 6.3% (12/190 attacks), accuracy 53.2%, FPR 0.0%, latency 355 ms
Llama Guard 3-8B (local, float16 CPU-offload on RTX A3000 6GB): F1 = 0.0105, precision 100.0%, recall 0.5% (1/190 attacks), accuracy 50.3%, FPR 0.0%, latency 46,767 ms
Internal ablation baseline: all-MiniLM-L6-v2 sentence-transformer embeddings substituted for ILION's 21-dim features -> F1 = 0.000
